body:
I actually think a giant PR adding ES6 features wherever possible, and updating the linter at the same time would be a bad idea. The way we should do it is like I'm doing here:

 - update the linter to accept es6 as a standalone commit
 - start using es6 features where they are useful
 - do a series of standalone commits that do one of two things:
   - adopt an ES6 feature where it is useful (e.g. const everything that can be consted), or
   - migrate a discrete chunk of code to using various useful ES6 features.

body:
Okay.

It looks like you are making an argument based on the lifetimes embedded in the `Idx` type of `trait Index<Idx>`.

I think I can see the reasoning there; its something like "the definition of the `Index` trait: `pub trait Index<Idx: ?Sized> { type Output: ?Sized; fn index(&self, index: Idx) -> &Self::Output; }`, will force any free lifetimes associated with the `Idx` to be bound at the scope of the `impl<'a, 'b, ...> Index<...> for Concrete<...>` for any concrete type `Concrete<...>`."

My follow-up question then is: Is there a case where the `Idx` type doesn't have any lifetimes in it, but the scope of temporaries of the expression computing `Idx` are nonetheless significant (and where the change suggested here could break code)?

My current *suspicion* is that the most natural cases where that could arise is that it could only happen in unsafe code that is *incorrect* (i.e., unsafe code that *should* be using lifetimes to express the constraints between the temporaries and the dynamic extent of the produced value, but is sidestepping doing so, and in the process opens up potential issues where user code can violate those now implicit constraints).
 * I think we have precedent for making changes like that, but I also think we need to be careful whenever we do so.
author: pnkfelix

body: There is not currently. Askama is a general-purpose text templating mechanism, and the "extra space" is only useless in the particular context of writing out HTML. I don't know the exact details of how reducing whitespace in a string serialization of the HTML DOM will affect that DOM, but if there is a reliable algorithm to implement that, I'd suggest you open an issue against Askama to discuss how we might support that. (For example, one way I might imagine doing it is by stripping any whitespace after a newline, but that doesn't remove all the whitespace the `{#- -#}` currently remove because it leaves the newlines themselves. However, if you strip the newlines themselves you would invalidate content like ``. So solving this optimally seems like a decently hard problem.)

body:
It's a pity that this can't really be `&'scope Scope<'env>` (I mean, it can, but it will, in practice, contradict the `+ 'env` requirement).

  - This whole design would thus be improved if non-explicitly-`move` closure were able to capture an `impl Copy` by move (and thus copy) rather than by ref, at least for some types that could opt into that behavior (cc @nikomatsakis, since you've put a lot of thought into improving closure captures, and cc @chorman0773). I posted lengthily about this on one of the original scoped-thread-API issues, but repeating the main point here as well to keep raising awareness 🙂
author: danielhenrymantilla

body:
My $.02 on this comes from an abi perspective, rather than a language perpsective, but seeing that I've been summoned , I may as well share it.
It may not necessarily be a good idea to capture *all* `Copy` types by move implicitly. A `[u32;1024]` may be trivial to copy, but it's certainly not cheap to copy or to store. 
In the preliminary abi spec used for [lccc](https://github.com/LightningCreations/lccc)'s rust frontend, I've prescribed that small (up to pointer-sized) values of a `Copy` type get stored in the closure directly if the address is unused.
> A capture of a type that is at most the same size of a pointer, which implements Copy and which is neither borrowed nor mutated by the body of the closure is captured by-copy for the purposes of ABI, even if the closure would otherwise capture it by-reference.

<https://lightningcreations.github.io/lccc/lcrust/abi/v0#closure-type-layout>

(Hilareously, this used to apply *all* Copy types, before that defect was pointed out to me).

It may or may not be a good idea to apply this rule as a language rule, mostly because it can be fairly interesting what "neither borrowed nor mutated" means (passing an `i32` capture to `println!`, for example, would exclude the capture from this rule in the abi, since the macro internally takes the address of each argument, despite not actually caring), and it would be a breaking change to extend it in general to all `Copy` captures regardless of whether the address is used (you could, for example, have `println!("{:p}",&i);` where `i` is an `i32` capture).

body:
Yeah, that's why I mentioned the opt-in part: I don't think there could be an always-applicable case; an alternative to annotating a `Copy` type its preference in being capture by move rather than by ref would be having the option to pin-point the captures of a closure:

```rust
scope.spawn(move(scope) || { // <- capture it if spawning nested threads
    scope.spawn(|| { … }); // otherwise unneeded
})
```

For the scoped threads API it would thus be less pretty than having that `move(scope)` happen implicitly, but it would be a more generally-applicable and visible alternative.

body:
The rationale for those was as follows:

If we have stored a successful allocation with `Reveal::Selection` then we can deduplicate that result on calls of `dedup_eval_alloc_raw` with both `Reveal::Selection` and `Reveal::UserFacing` (since `Selection` and `UserFacing` only differ in that we keep certain errors silent with `Selection`, but successful allocations are always equivalent). This is also the reason why we can deduplicate from saved calls with `UserFacing`on calls with both `Selection` and `UserFacing`. 

If we try to deduplicate with `Reveal::All` we should be able to deduplicate from saved query results with both `Selection` and `UserFacing`, since `All` will always succeed if either `UserFacing` or `Selection` have succeeded. On the other hand if we have a saved result with `Reveal::All`, it's not guaranteed that query calls with `Reveal::Selection` or `Reveal::UserFacing` succeed, so we can't deduplicate from those.

body:
A `FragmentKind` represents some sort of "projection" out of a type. So, since fields are conceptually "children" of a type, as are variants and associated items, they get a `FragmentKind`.

The ultimate reason why `FragmentKind` exists is that rustdoc only generates independent pages for some kinds of items. For example, structs and free functions get their own pages, but fields, variants, and associated items are just sections on their parent type's page. In most places, this is captured by returning a `Res` for the page and a `FragmentKind` representing the section within that page. Then the `Res` is turned into a path like `../foo/struct.Bar.html` and the `FragmentKind` is turned into a [URL fragment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/URI_fragment) like `#structfield.baz`.

Does that explanation help?

body:
I don't find it clear what "stored in order" means for a data structure like this. They're stored in a tree structure which is not contiguous in memory. In practice it's easy to have greater elements (according to Ord) stored at lower addresses.

I would prefer this documentation not to say anything about how the data is stored. It can say what guarantees are made by the iterator, but it's the iterator's job to bridge between how the stuff is stored (which is a private detail) and how they're sequenced when accessed by the user of the data structure.

Performance characteristics (Big O?) are also fine to document. That should hopefully substitute for some of the desire to document "how it's stored".

body:
The problem with doing normalizing like this, is that maybe one type *can* be normalized, but the other can't. We probably should allow coercing by relating the projection itself.

Another problem with doing it like this: what happens if the result of a normalization is itself a projection. You could imagine that we might want to be able to coerce to any types in the "chain" of normalizations from either side. But we can't just *relate* types, because we also allow e.g. unsizing. Otherwise, lazy norm might be able to solve this.

Imo, the other half of this PR (not constraining inference vars when confirming a method call) is "more correct" than this one. There might be some version of this change that can be more conservative, where we could just require a `::<T, U>` to be specified.
author: jackh726

body: Problem here: We don't have a way to "project_and_unify_type" for generalized type (essentially a `Relate` instead of a `Fold`). This hack fails as soon as we wrap the projection in an adt, for example.

regarding the other question, `Foreign` which is a `TyKind` has derived `Eq`, and as far as I understand about derived `Eq` of algebraic data types, the implementation is just applying `Eq` to all the fields of that type. author: nagisa

body:
When `--cfg a(b=c)` or similarly malformed argument is provided, such as in the test below, this added note seems really out of place.

Is there a way rustc could detect specifically for the situation where the escapes may be missing? I imagine any case where this situation occurs, rustc will see something like `key=value`.

Alternatively what about if rustc printed out the argument we received and could not parse? Something like

```
expected `key` or `key="value"`, got `key=value`
```

seem like might be just enough information for the user to figure out what may be going wrong (and avoids assuming any details about the environment)
author: nagisa

body:
Currently, the value the parser obtained is already in the output:
```
$ rustc --cfg foo=val
error: invalid `--cfg` argument: `foo=val` (expected `key` or `key="value"`)
```
But, this unfortunately doesn't do much in guiding the user towards the escaping issue. If one is to read the message carefully, you can spot the difference of course. But if spotting the difference was easy, the issue wouldn't have been filed. Neither would I have gone through the trouble to incorporate its [suggestion](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/66450#issuecomment-554457074) to more clearly nudge towards the proper escaping. 

Stating what we received duplicates this information, it does more closely couple the quoted `"value"` next to whatever the user passed in though, so the difference may be easier to spot? It would look like this: 
```
$ ./rustc --cfg foo=val
error: invalid `--cfg` argument: `foo=val` (expected `key` or `key="value"`, got `foo=val`)
```

I definitely agree with you that the current message would look odd in a lot of situations, like when a shell variable substitution goes wrong or something;
```
$ rustc --cfg '$VAR'
error: invalid `--cfg` argument: `$VAR` (expected `key` or `key="value"`, ensure escaping is appropriate for your shell, try 'key="value"' or key=\"value\")
```
Like, in that case it's just the wrong advice, I didn't really think of that failure mode. Basically if any invalid character occurs in the string, we would get this 'hint' now, even though it may not be applicable, this is definitely not desirable.

So thinking about this a bit more, we can easily make the error message more specific by checking for `=` and if that is present check for `="` and ending with `"`. That makes this hint much more specific, only hinting in cases where the user likely tried to do a `key="value"` cfg flag? We will still trigger on the `a(b=c)` situation, but I think that's reasonable? The `(` can't be part of the key, the `)` could be part of the value; `ab="c)"` is allowed.

I've implemented this limiting the scope of printing this hint using the simple rules proposed, see the updated unit tests. Only the `a(b=c)` and (newly added) `key=value` unit tests now print this hint, let me know your thoughts.

author: ssomers

body:
Oh, the Leakpocalypse is quite old news, that I probably read years ago. Yes, definitely related to this PR.

My concern above (and [steffahn](https://github.com/steffahn)'s in #93768) is for the flexibility of the library developer. But personally, I think that's unproductive. If you write in the contract that hell may descend upon earth in some conditions, when that never actually happens, people are still going to write client software drifting into those conditions. When you, the library developer, later change the implementation and raise hell to exploit the freedom that the contract awards you, the client software breaks. You can say it's the user's fault, but it's the language's reputation you've messed with.

So here is my own suggestion for a more user-centric wording of the "Leaking" topic for `vec::drain`, while still giving a bit of leeway to the implementation:

**If the returned iterator goes out of scope without being dropped (due to `mem::forget`, for example), the vector may have lost and leaked an arbitrary number of elements, including those outside the range.**
author: ssomers

body:
> Not sure what you mean with "this". I for one hadn't heard about this Leakpocalypse.

I'm sorry, I shouldn't have assumed, "this" being the leak behavior of `Drain`. The "Leakpocalypse" was an event around 2015 when it was widely realized that various RAII style guard types that were being used to enforce soundness on APIs such as scoped threads and older versions of `Drain` were infact unsound since they could be trivially leaked via a reference cycle. I am not sure but I think prior to this `mem::leak` was either unsafe or maybe didn't even exist, not sure. I wasn't a Rust user back then. Regardless, the blog post I linked goes into the problem in detail and describes the mitigations they put in place, colloquially dubbed "pre-pooping your pants" using `DrainRange` as the basis of the explanation. In this case when you create a `DrainRange` it pre-emptively leaks every element including the ones past the end of the range. Then when you drop it normally it drops any leftover elements and then backshifts all the remaining elements, but if you leak the iterator and never drop it the elements are still already leaked and using the original collection will show those elements as gone already, preventing use after free.

> Which I guess implies the collection shrinks, but I'd still call that out. But my main question is: should or must? We're describing a contract that can't be backed out of.

must, I thiink, though I'm not positive I understand what you mean with that question. When I say it "should result" I meant to say "it does result". If we wanted to describe this explicitly we'd probably add a `# Current Implementation` section so we can describe what we currently do without necessarily making an API commitment that would prevent us from changing it in the future if we found a better way to keep `Drain` sound in the presence of `mem::forget`.

>  I wonder if there could ever be more damage than elements disappearing. I don't think it would ever be acceptable that it results in duplicate elements, double frees, panics, aborts or non-termination, but is that all that could happen? I can imagine a future desire to condone ugliness if the container is used later in anything but dropping.

As far as I know though none of those issues you listed are possible to invoke with safe usage of `Drain` with the current implementation. If you have reason to think there may be bugs that we haven't caught that should absolutely be fixed, but that's probably best done in a separate issue. As things stand right now I believe that if you construct a `Drain` and then immediately forget it you get left with a container of the same capacity but empty, where all of the elements that were previously stored in the container are now treated as uninitialized memory and will be overwritten without invoking their drop impls as new elements are pushed into the container.

> But it is pretty much a copy in this PR.

It's possible I'm confused, but from what I can see it is not a copy. These are the sections I'm referring to:

```diff
- Please don't skip dropping this iterator (using [`mem::forget`], for example).
- This may well leave the heap in an unexpected state, with surprising behavior
- when you continue using it afterwards, and leaks an arbitrary number of
- elements.

+ When the iterator **is** dropped, all elements in the range are removed from
+ the collection, even if the iterator was not fully consumed. If the iterator **is
+ not** dropped (with [`mem::forget`] for example), it is unspecified how many
+ elements are removed.
```
author: yaahc

body:
> "leave the heap in an unexpected state"

Now I realize that this could be interpreted as the program's heap, not the binary heap that the method is applied to. Not sure if that's what you meant, but a newcomer might poop their pants coming across it.

> must, I think, though I'm not positive I understand what you mean with that question.

I think you understood it as well as I did.  So the description is a commitment to the library user and a constraint on the future library developer. But, like any contract, that still doesn't mean it can't ever be broken. Clearly, if there is no description of leaking at all, like in an ancient vec::drain and currently in binary heap, that doesn't mean that the only weirdness ever allowed is an obvious "unconsumed drained elements are leaked".

> If we wanted to describe this explicitly we'd probably add a `# Current Implementation` section so we can describe what we currently do without necessarily making an API commitment

I like that idea. Because I think those running into situations caused by leak, are not going to cater for every possibility covered by their interpretation of the method's description. They're going to look up what happens now and cater for that.

> > I wonder if there could ever be more damage than elements disappearing. I don't think it would ever be acceptable that it results in duplicate elements, double frees, panics, aborts or non-termination, but is that all that could happen? I can imagine a future desire to condone ugliness if the container is used later in anything but dropping.
> 
> As far as I know though none of those issues you listed are possible to invoke with safe usage of `Drain` with the current implementation.

I'm not suggesting they are possible now, and I guess something like double frees would be "undefined behaviour" anyway, just wondering how much worse vec::drain might ever become.

> As things stand right now I believe that if you construct a `Drain` and then immediately forget it you get left with a container of the same capacity but empty, where all of the elements that were previously stored in the container are now treated as uninitialized memory and will be overwritten without invoking their drop impls as new elements are pushed into the container.

For `Vec`, yes (also if you don't immediately forget it). For `String`, the drained characters magically reappear. For `VecDeque`, I think it's like `Vec` but I'm not sure. For `BinaryHeap`, I'm now pretty sure there's no issue since a heap is just a wrapper around a `Vec` and we only drain the full range (so I'm taking the bit that inspired this thread out). `drain_sorted` is another matter ~~but I already backed out on editing that description~~ (EDIT: and apparently later I backed back in again).

> > But it is pretty much a copy in this PR.
> 
> It's possible I'm confused, but from what I can see it is not a copy.

This PR is a direct attack on `vec::drain`'s current description because of the reasons listed in #92765. So instead getting me to spread it elsewhere would be a feat indeed. Are you up for the challenge?

To be clarify, I mean it is `insert_fit`which returns a `*mut V` instead of `&mut V` in the fact that it *can* returns a `&mut V`.
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/3d1eaf4b6203ab0168da5b99049942385aa753e0/library/alloc/src/collections/btree/node.rs#L845-L856

> insert unfortunately can't return a &mut V because that would be a lie: it's not an exclusive reference since the function also returns an InsertResult that points to the same key-value pair.

Sure, I agree with you about `Handle::insert`.

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/3d1eaf4b6203ab0168da5b99049942385aa753e0/library/alloc/src/collections/btree/node.rs#L623-L632
As you can see, `push` does what `insert_fit` does just like `Vec::push` does what `Vec::insert` does, except the former inserts at the end, and the latter inserts inside the node.

 I guess the reason why `insert_fit` returns a `*mut V` instead of `&mut V` is, that its caller `Handle::insert` (which returns an `InsertResult` with a `*mut V`) actually needs a `*mut V`, and even given a `&mut V`, the caller should also cast it to a `*mut V` because of the reason you have just mentioned. So does `push` with its caller `Entry::insert`.

Even though `Entry::insert` returns a `&mut V` in the end, it cannot preserve the returned `&mut V` since the temporarily created root node has to be moved into `map.root`. Our borrow checker doesn't allow that.

body: This lack of optimisation for the empty case is a bit of a worry in terms of whether `collect_with` is a good API at all. Wouldn't all of the good usages be better covered by the mutable `collect_into`? I had envisioned we could use `.collect_with(Vec::new())` to replace the turbofish, but it seems that is almost never a good idea, or at least only applicable in certain circumstances. Now we would be creating a more ergonomic API whose use we would have to immediately discourage.
author: cormacrelf

body:
`collect_with` can be very useful in order to avoid reallocation(s) when `size_hint` returns the default value.

> I had envisioned we could use .collect_with(Vec::new()) to replace the turbofish, but it seems that is almost never a good idea, or at least only applicable in certain circumstances.

I agree that it is almost never a good idea. I also like more the turbofish syntax where applicable.  

My idea for `collect_with` was a method to be used when `size_hint` is not helpful and the collection (in which the items are collected) does not already exists. This allows shorter code:

```rust
let len1 = vec![1, 2, 3]
    .into_iter()
    .filter(|&x| x < 3)
    .collect_with(Vec::with_capacity(2))
    .len();

// Instead of
let mut vec = Vec::with_capacity(2);
let len2 = vec![1, 2, 3]
    .into_iter()
    .filter(|&x| x < 3)
    .collect_into(&mut vec)
    .len();
```

I think we could specify it in the doc. However, someone could still misuse the method. Calling `Extend::extend_reserve` should, at least, fix the speed gap between `collect()` and misused `collect_with`. (not already implemented)

Eventually, I realised `collect_with` can be implemented with a call to `collect_into`, should it be a better implementation?

This discussion is about whether the `ino` is sufficient or we also need the `dev` when we're ascending out of a directory by opening `..`. Within a device moving would change the `ino`. By attacking the `..` and redirecting it into a sticky bit directory (commonly `/tmp`) would mean a less privileged attacker could trick a more privileged process into deleting stuff owned by other users because they can manipulate stuff in a directory that also contains stuff owned by other users.
I'm not sure if this is actually exploitable due to the restrictions on mount moving, but maybe it's possible with namespaces and a more privileged process entering some namespaces of a less privileged process. Maybe as an alternative to the `..` traversal we could keep an exponentially spaced set of parent file descriptors.
I.e. 

```rust
struct DirComponent {
  name: CString,
  fd: Option<CachedReadDir>,
}
```

Descending deeper into the tree would require occasionally dropping cached entries closer to the root. Ascending would occasionally have to reopen more than one intermediate directory to restore the right distribution. Close to the current directory we could also keep a dense set.
author: the8472

body:
As long as the number of cached file descriptors is constant, the number of fs operations required is O(depth^2) for the adversarial case. With the implementation in this PR we can delete a million nested subdirs in ~16 seconds (just like `rm -r`, which does something similar according to strace).

So far I haven't seen a plausible threat scenario for this implementation. So I suggest we spend some time to find one and unless we do find one to go with this implementation. I am particularly concerned about the inode reuse case and normal `mv` operations.

Using the device id + inode for comparison and hashing to avoid loops could be done as additional safeguards, even though we haven't proven that they are needed. I haven't looked at the `openat2()` syscall, but if that offers additional safeguards we could use it as well. Edit: `openat2` is kernel 5.6+, so we definitely need to check availability. ~~FreeBSD has `O_RESOLVE_BENEATH`.~~ (not suitable)

body:
> Exponential spacing implies log(depth) + C ancestors being kept rather than a constant amount.

Oh I thought you just meant spacing out a constant number of cached parents differently. That makes more sense.

The inode reuse case could also be exploited within the same filesystem. But lots of things would have to line up for that to work IMHO:
1. While `remove_dir_all("/home/attacker/foo")` is proceeding with elevated privileges in the depths, say in `home/attacker/foo/bar/baz`....
2. The attacker moves `/home/attacker/foo/bar` to a temporary place, say `/home/attacker/my_tmp/bar` and deletes the now empty parent directory `/home/attacker/foo` freeing its inode.
3. The inode is reused (very common on Linux at least with ext4) by a privileged process for a directory with the sticky bit set, e.g. for `/newtmp`.
4. The attacker moves `/home/attacker/my_tmp/bar` to `/newtmp/bar`.
5. `remove_dir_all` ascends into the sticky dir inode check succeeding and proceeds to delete other entries in it.

That is the best I have come up with so far.
author: hkratz

body:
Interesting! Waiting until a different privileged process (not necessarily the one that does the recursive deleting) does something adds extra power.

> But lots of things would have to line up for that to work IMHO

An attacker may be able to stall the privileged process indefinitely by using multiple threads or io_uring to keep filling the tree faster than it's being deleted. It can then use that time to arrange whatever constellation of inodes it needs. Perhaps it can even arrange a suid process to be spawned with a lower CPU/IO priority or even in a cgroup controlled by the attacker that'll allow it to be frozen. This way it could wait until an opportunity arises. Then it wouldn't even show up in the system load.

body:
This should be ok now. When going up with `openat(dir_fd, b"..")`we compare the dev/inode and also open the grandparent checking its dev/inode. That way we can be reasonably sure to detect if the parent has been sneakily substituted and happens to have the same inode.

An attacker would need to get a priviliged process to trace to create a dir/subdir pair with subdir being sticky and dir/subdir having the expected inodes in order for this to be exploitable. And if that were possible it could only force the victim to delete stuff that has already been created in there. Can't be much since it is created in a race.

To be honest I am not even sure if that additional precaution is even necessary, because without that check we would have the same security as gnulib fts used in coreutils rm and the damage would still be contained due to it only working with directory inode reuse. That naturally limits the amount of damage to files created in the the time between the attacker deleting the directory and the superuser creating a new (sticky) dir with the same inode and filling it with sensitive content.

Is there a reason for this test?

We don't normally test that `#[unstable]` library features are actually unstable. If we'd do that for every unstable macro/type/function/etc., that would quickly add a lot of unnecessary tests, making everybody wait even longer when they wait for the ui tests.

I don't think we should do this. We usually don't touch `rustc_feature` at all for unstable library features, and there are already plenty of unstable features that could be used for unsoundness when enabled. We shouldn't try to attach any soundness/safety guarantee to unstable features, as that just raises the expectations of unstable features. The feature being permanently unstable, having `unsafe` in the name, and having `internals` in the name, is more than enough.

(Also, this produces a weird error about a feature being incompatible with itself.)

author: m-ou-se

body:
> there are already plenty of unstable features that could be used for unsoundness when enabled

There are? Interesting. FWIW, as far as I know, for *language* features soundness bugs even in unstable features are considered a problem, and such features are typically listed as "incomplete feature". On the language level, it is *not* considered okay to have unsoundness when enabling a feature gate.

Looks like T-libs-api and T-lang have different policies here?
author: RalfJung

body:
I remember that a few things around specialization resulted in unsoundness in the standard library. While it did involve an attribute with `unsafe_` in the name and some code making assumptions about it, these do seem to be features where the soundness story isn't fully understood yet, without these features being marked as 'incomplete'.

I'm also reminded of the (recently removed) unstable `std::io::Initializer`, which entire purpose was to simplify working with uninitialized `&mut [u8]` buffers, resulting in unsoundness. Cases like these are a lot more subtle than the field in `Pin` of course, and it still involves `unsafe` functions.

Recently we also had `Pin::static_ref` as `unstable`. Before our discussion and the FCP blessing that, that was also technically unsound as you argued on that tracking issue.

(It'd be nice if we could mark the *field* of `Pin` as unsafe, but totally not worth it for just a single use case. In almost all use cases, an `unsafe` constructor function is fine.)

All these cases are much more subtle than the one in this PR though. Maybe the unsound unstable features situation isn't as bad as I thought. Still, I would still want to be able to experiment with unstable features in the library for which we're not entirely sure yet what the soundness story is (as long as we properly document that, of course).
author: m-ou-se

body:
Would marking it as incomplete rather than this "incompatible with itself" be a decent middle-ground, then?

### The soundness contract

For me two things have to be distinguished <sub><sup>(note: I'll be talking of `incomplete_feature`s as the ones representing `potentially_unsound_feature`s (which would otherwise be a superset of it), since the latter do not exist yet. But feel free to imagine there being such `potentially_unsound_feature`s, in which case just replace my mentions to the former with mentions to the latter)</sup></sub>:

  - potentially unsound features, such as specialization, `std::io::Initializer`, _etc._,

    which I'd _personally_ like to be, at least, marked as `incomplete` (since the warning there does mention potential lack of soundness);

  - and the believed-to-be-sound features, even if they later appear to be unsound (at which point they'd either be removed, fixed, or would fall into the previous category). I'd put pre-FCPed-`Pin::static_ref`[^1] in this category, for instance.

[^1]: Very interesting read, that was, btw!

Pros/cons of this approach:

  - :x: More maintenance burden for compiler / libs authors, which may look surprising when we are talking of `unstable`-gated stuff, whose purpose is quite tied to "avoiding" / minimizing that.

    I'd personally hypothesize this may not be that bad / be manageable, since we could be very lenient _w.r.t._ tagging features as `potentially_unsound`.

  - :white_check_mark: The benefit is more trust _w.r.t._ `nightly` APIs / less incentive to mistrust `nightly` altogether in highly-safety-concerned scenarios:

    assume a `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` codebase (in a transitive fashion, with carefully reviewed allow-listed exceptions, such as the stdlib itself). With this approach, by adding a (similarly transitive) `#![forbid(incomplete_features)]`, they'd be able to "trust" the lack of UB in their codebase, _modulo bugs in the trusted dependencies_, such as the language itself, or the stdlib.

    By not banning `nightly` `#![feature]`s altogether, this codebase would still be able to use things such as [`Result::copied()`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.58.1/std/result/enum.Result.html#method.copied), **which is currently `unstable`, but harmless**: without a (lint-enabled) way to distinguish between `Result::copied` and access to the `.pointer` field of `Pin`, such codebases might have to maintain their own allow-list of the "harmless" `nightly` features, or _ban all of nightly altogether_.

Now, I really don't care what the distinguisher is, provided it can be linted against: `incomplete_features`, `potentially_unsound_features`, `internal_features` (maybe this last one expresses the right intent _w.r.t._ maintainability burden?).

In that regard, _naming_, in and of itself, is already a (slightly brittle) form of distinction: with `unsafe` and `internal` in the name, I agree that qualifying it as unsound sounds a bit silly / hypocrite (even if I can also see it as legitimate).

### Back to this PR

That's why I'll settle either way, although I'd _personally_ lean towards, _at least_, keeping the feature tagged as `incomplete` (even if that technically makes it become a `lang` feature rather than a lib one).

body:
Extra nit, but I personally would love that level of precision in the diagnostics: in the case where `P = &mut _`, that is, when we have to deal with `Pin<&mut …>`, we already have a borrow; and what we need is a _**re**-borrow_.

  - This may look silly, but I think one of the main areas of confusion with `Pin` for beginners is this need for `.as_mut()`, precisely because it's a type which, contrary to `&mut`, features no implicit reborrowing.

    So even _an extra **note**_ for that very case, stating this very fact, could be quite educational:

    ```console
    note: this function takes ownership of the receiver `self`, which moves `foo`
      --> $DIR/pin-content-move.rs:6:12
       |
    LL |     fn foo(self: Pin<&mut Self>) {}
       |            ^^^^
    help: consider calling `.as_mut()` to reborrow the type's contents
       |
    LL |     foo.as_mut().foo();
       |         +++++++++
    note: this is needed because `Pin<&mut _>`, contrary to `&mut _`, does not feature implicit reborrowing.
       |
    LL |     foo.as_mut().foo();
       |         +++++++++
    ```

  - While that instance seems like a nit, in cases such as `Pin<Rc<…>>` _etc._ for shared-pinned stuff, the `.as_mut()` suggestion is also likely to be incorrect.

    So, if it weren't too annoying to implement, I'd do a heuristic whereby `Rc`, `Arc`, and `&mut _` would be special-cased, and would ask for, respectively: `.as_ref()` to _borrow_, `.as_ref()` to _borrow_, `.as_mut()` to _reborrow_, and otherwise defaulting to what we have here: `.as_mut()` to _borrow_.

`quiet` is from https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/commit/5cf300d695c4ac6e4bdab8fe5c48de6b05b2cd96 from #84176.

@GuillaumeGomez do you know if that change can be reverted? It shouldn't have been merged, it makes it look like it's legitimate to disable diagnostics (to be clear, this is very dangerous, especially as more things rely on `delay_span_bug` and error emission).

There is no way to keep `rustdoc` doing what that PR does and still e.g. create `ty::Error`. An error *has to* be emitted to have `ty::Error`.

I don't think compromising the integrity of the rest of the system is worth getting access to method call annotations in code, which seems to be the only use of this misfeature.

AFAICT the only way to get the information you want is to run a *separate* compilation pass, and sinkhole errors at the actual point where they would be printed to `stderr`, while letting the compiler properly track the fact that errors happened, and then scavenge whatever data is available, despite those errors.

body:
> `INVALID_HANDLE_VALUE` aliases the current process handle

Well, they are totally different types. No one would try to pass `io::RawHandle` where `io::RawSocket` is expected or where a _process handle_ is expected.
author: pravic

body:
In Windows, the current process handle, as returned by  [`GetCurrentProcess`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/processthreadsapi/nf-processthreadsapi-getcurrentprocess#remarks) has type `HANDLE`, aka `io::RawHandle` here, which *is* the same as the return type of eg. [`CreateFileW`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/fileapi/nf-fileapi-createfilew).

`SOCKET`, aka `io::RawSocket` here, on the other hand, is a different type.
author: sunfishcode

body:
And so does a dozens of other types that are also an alias (or a [newtype](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3429523/what-does-this-line-of-c-c-preprocessor-mean) if compiled with `-DSTRICT`) of `HANDLE`: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/winprog/windows-data-types

So, in fact it should be a newtype in Rust as well.

Okay, to be more formal: while both `GetCurrentProcess` and `CreateFile` (and `CreateEvent`, etc) have the same types (`HANDLE`), in fact they are different handle types and no one familiar with winapi would try to use them interchangeably.
author: pravic

body:
Some of the types linked there using `HANDLE` are so different that they don't even use `CloseHandle`. We already do document that it's not valid to use `OwnedHandle`/`BorrowedHandle` to hold those kinds of handles.

Beyond that though, `OwnedHandle`, `BorrowedHandle`, and `AsHandle` are intended to be used with both file handles and process handles, for three reasons:
 - Rust already uses the same `RawHandle`/`AsRawHandle`/`IntoRawHandle` types and traits for both [processes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/process/struct.Child.html#impl-AsRawHandle) and [files](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/fs/struct.File.html#impl-AsRawHandle), so it's consistent with existing APIs in Rust
 - Both process handles and file handles can be passed to `CloseHandle`, `GetHandleInformation`, `SetHandleInformation`, `DuplicateHandle`, and others, so they may be different, but they're not completely different.
 - Rust also does have distinct higher-level types for different kinds of handles, such as `std::fs::File` for file(-like) handles, and `std::process::Child` for process handles, and these are what most Rust code uses most of the time, so it isn't as important to make a static type distinction at the `OwnedHandle`/etc. level.

body:
This overall seems good to me -- definitely a confusing function that seems likely to be at least partially wrong in many use cases.

It looks like the majority of the clippy users arose as a result of https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/pull/5528, which seems like it's under the impression that this function was *intentional* -- but I admit I am not fully following the conversation there. Maybe worth considering whether we need to provide a lightweight alternative that does whatever clippy was going for? cc @phansch @flip1995 

The uses in this PR seem like they are probably OK with the "full" equality proposed by this PR, but it's hard to be sure. I guess tests are passing (including Clippy tests), though. I think it'd be good to get another set of eyes on these changes (particularly those in Clippy) before merging -- maybe r? @jackh726

body:
😆 this is your code, we just accidentally took it over and I didn't realize at all what was going on.

Since we require 

> x != y implies hash_stable(x) != hash_stable(y)

for stable hashing, but any info in `local_id_to_def_id` is dependent on the body, then just hashing the body satisfies this condition.

author: wesleywiser

body:
To be clear: Wesley's assumption is that the unused `mut` lint started firing here (because the mutation of `result` falls after the call to `never()` below, and so that's why the PR author has removed the `mut`.

And Wesley's question is: Do we need to worry about this? Considering e.g. code that might have `#![deny(unused_mut)]`, and now it starts seeing that as an error.

(My personal answer is that lints are allowed to change their behavior in this manner. I'm currently double-checking/asking around for examples from Rust's past.)
author: pnkfelix

body: Additional discussion in https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/213817-t-lang/topic/lints.2C.20flow-sensitivity.2C.20and.20stability
author: wesleywiser

body:
The lint side of things seems fine to me from compatibility perspective. It is
not unusual for us to adjust how lints work, and practical impact is limited by
cargo capping the lint level for dependencies.

The fact that we would now accept more programs seems more significant, since
it would be a breaking change to revert. Though, as far as I can see, we did in
fact accept those programs after #54125 landed, but have since regressed after
switching to MIR borrowck.
author: tmiasko

body: This is a somewhat unfortunate reduction in test coverage for validity (we now error much earlier during evaluation)... ideally this would be adjusted to transmute to a type that is not 'visibly' uninhabited so that we still test the same code paths as before.
author: RalfJung

body: It also seems surprising that this `mut` would be unused, since `result` *is* mutated in later code -- in a regular function, we are not that smart wrt unused-mut warnings, or are we?
author: RalfJung

body:
I was talking about this with @scottmcm a little while back and he found this example: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=26220b66188903b1dd8f3325960f75af

That version compiles even though `x` is mutated, since the mutation comes after the panic. Although, for me making it `let mut x` doesn't raise an unused mut warning. Anyway, I think this shows prior art for being clever about things being mutated only in unreachable code, so this change seems to make things more uniform.
